questions.question: Explain the concept of paging in operating systems. What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Paging divides logical memory into fixed-size pages and physical memory into frames
Eliminates external fragmentation but causes internal fragmentation
Simple memory protection through page-level access bits
Provides fast address translation with page tables
Disadvantages: internal fragmentation, page table overhead, cannot provide logical view

questions.question: What is a page fault? Explain the steps involved in handling a page fault.
keyPoints: Page fault occurs when a referenced page is not in physical memory
Steps: 1) Trap to OS, 2) Check validity of reference, 3) Locate page on disk, 4) Find free frame, 5) Read page from disk, 6) Update page table, 7) Restart the interrupted instruction
Page fault handling involves I/O and context switching overhead

questions.question: Explain the Working Set model for controlling thrashing.
keyPoints: Working set is the set of pages a process is currently using
The model tries to keep active pages in memory to prevent thrashing
If working set size > available frames, some processes are suspended
Thrashing occurs when total working set exceeds physical memory
WSClock algorithm implements this model

questions.question: Differentiate between paging and segmentation.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size pages, physical view, eliminates external fragmentation
Segmentation: variable-sized segments, logical view, enables sharing and protection
Paging has internal fragmentation, segmentation has external fragmentation
Logical address: paging uses (page#, offset), segmentation uses (segment#, offset)
Segmentation provides better protection and natural code/data separation

questions.question: Explain the purpose and working of TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer).
keyPoints: TLB is a hardware cache for page table entries
Provides fast virtual-to-physical address translation
Contains recently used page-frame mappings
On TLB miss, page table is accessed and entry is loaded into TLB
Associative memory (parallel search) for fast lookup
Hit ratio determines performance

questions.question: What is Belady's Anomaly? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Belady's anomaly is the phenomenon where increasing frames increases page faults in FIFO
Demonstrates that some page replacement algorithms don't follow intuition
Example: with 3 frames: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 gives 9 faults; with 4 frames: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 gives 10 faults
Does not occur in LRU or Optimal algorithms
Caused by FIFO not considering page usage frequency

questions.question: Explain demand paging and compare it with prepaging.
keyPoints: Demand paging loads pages only when referenced (on-demand)
Reduces initial load time and memory usage
Prepaging loads pages in advance before they are needed
Demand paging may cause initial page faults
Prepaging may waste memory if pages not used
Hybrid approach often used in practice

questions.question: Explain the following page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU, and Optimal. Which one is practically implementable and why?
keyPoints: FIFO: Removes oldest page in memory - simple but suffers from Belady's anomaly
LRU: Removes least recently used page - based on temporal locality, better performance but needs hardware support
Optimal: Removes page that will not be used for longest time - theoretical best, requires future knowledge
LRU can be approximated using reference bits and counters
Clock algorithm provides practical LRU approximation
FIFO is simplest but not always best; LRU is practical with hardware support
Optimal cannot be implemented but serves as benchmark

questions.question: Explain the combined paging and segmentation memory management technique. How does address translation work in such a system?
keyPoints: Combines advantages of both paging and segmentation
Logical address: segment number, page number within segment, and page offset
Segment table points to page table for each segment
Two-level translation: segment→page table, then page→frame
Provides logical view (segments) and efficient memory management (paging)
Protection can be at segment level and page level
Reduces external fragmentation from segmentation, internal from small pages
Address format: [Segment#][Page#][Offset]

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain its causes, symptoms, and the techniques used to prevent or recover from thrashing.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive page faults causing CPU underutilization and poor performance
Causes: too many processes, insufficient frames, poor locality
Symptoms: high paging I/O, low CPU utilization, declining throughput
Prevention: Working Set model, Page Fault Frequency approach, Process suspension
Working set maintains minimum frames per process
Page Fault Frequency adjusts allocation based on fault rate
OS can suspend or swap out low priority processes
Proper frame allocation algorithms help prevent thrashing

questions.question: Explain the different types of page tables used in virtual memory systems.
keyPoints: Hierarchical Paging: multi-level page tables, good for large address spaces
Inverted Page Table: one entry per physical frame, saves memory but slow lookup
Hashed Page Table: uses hashing for fast lookup, handles large spaces
Segmented Page Table: combines segment table with page tables
Advantages/disadvantages: memory vs speed tradeoff
Forward mapping page tables: simple but large for big address spaces
Inverted reduces memory significantly for large systems
Modern systems use hierarchical with TLB for best performance
